chunk_summary: This section outlines conditions where breastfeeding is contraindicated, including infant metabolic disorders, maternal infections, and substance use. It emphasizes infection control and lactation protection during temporary interruptions.
learning_objectives: Identify absolute and relative contraindications to breastfeeding in infants and mothers.
Understand management strategies for breastfeeding interruptions due to maternal treatment or infections.
Learn infection-control measures for breastfeeding during maternal illnesses like COVID-19 or herpetic lesions.
Recognize the importance of maintaining lactation through scheduled expression when breastfeeding is interrupted.
key_facts: Classic galactosemia is an absolute contraindication to breastfeeding; lactose-free formula is required.
Maternal cytotoxic chemotherapy and therapeutic radioisotope use necessitate breastfeeding interruption with a restart plan.
Maternal HTLV-1/2 infection is a contraindication in many guidelines.
Active herpetic lesions on the breast require avoiding feeding from the affected breast until lesions resolve.
For infections like treated tuberculosis, hepatitis, and COVID-19, breastfeeding is generally recommended with infection-control measures.
Scheduled expression (8–10 times per day) is critical to protect lactation during interruptions.
reference_quote: For most common infections (including treated tuberculosis, hepatitis viruses, and COVID-19), breastfeeding or expressed milk is recommended with infection-control measures and infant prophylaxis as indicated; therefore, automatic separation should be avoided. (CDC, Breastfeeding special circumstances)
document_key_topics: Infant metabolic disorders and breastfeeding contraindications
